nativeRange.habitat: Coastal brackish estuaries, tidal rivers, and slow-moving lowland streams; also enters adjacent freshwater reaches; bottom-oriented in shallow, often turbid water with sandy or muddy substrate and emergent vegetation

decorPreferences: rocks and caves creating nooks and crannies
hardy plants (Java fern, Anubias, mangrove root)
driftwood for cover and territory definition
crushed coral or aragonite substrate optional (buffers pH and KH)

diet.notes: The Figure Eight Puffer is a strict carnivore that requires hard-shelled prey to wear down its continuously growing fused teeth, often called the beak. Without regular hard-shelled food the beak overgrows, eventually preventing the fish from feeding. Core staples include live or frozen snails (bladder, ramshorn, and nerite varieties are all suitable), mussels, cockles, oyster meat, krill, and ghost shrimp. Frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, bloodworms, and mosquito larvae work well as supplementary variety. Dried and pellet foods are not generally accepted. Offering live snails two to three times per week is strongly advised to keep the beak worn down to a healthy length. Care should be taken not to overfeed, as puffers tend to continue eating well past satiety.

goodWith: bumblebee gobies (Brachygobius spp.) in brackish setups
avoidWith: slow-moving or long-finned fish (severe fin-nipping risk)
conspecifics without ample space and visual breaks
Green Spotted Puffer (Dichotomyctere nigroviridis — much larger and more aggressive)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Tetraodon biocellatus (synonym). Check current availability on FishBase summary page.

summary: The Figure Eight Puffer is a small Southeast Asian brackish-water fish reaching about 8 cm, named for the yellow figure-eight markings on its back. Though often sold as a freshwater species, it tends to fare significantly better in low-end brackish conditions (specific gravity 1.005–1.010). Regular hard-shelled prey is essential to wear down its continuously growing fused teeth.
careNotes:
The Figure Eight Puffer is among the most widely kept brackish-water aquarium fish, prized for its bold personality, apparent recognition of owners, and entertaining feeding behaviour. Despite being frequently sold and labelled as a freshwater species, it is fundamentally euryhaline (able to tolerate a range of salinities) — native to coastal tidal estuaries and brackish rivers of Southeast Asia — and the consensus among experienced keepers is that long-term health is significantly better in low-end brackish water (specific gravity 1.005–1.010) than in pure freshwater. Specimens maintained in pure freshwater often survive for a year or two but tend to suffer elevated disease rates and shortened lifespans; at a specific gravity of 1.005–1.008, lifespans of 10–15 years have been documented.

A minimum tank of 75 L (20 gal US) with a 75 cm footprint suits one specimen. An additional 20–30 L per extra fish is needed if attempting group housing, which requires a spacious, heavily decorated tank with multiple visual breaks. Because this species is widely regarded as an aggressive fin-nipper, only fast-swimming, short-finned brackish tankmates such as bumblebee gobies and salt-tolerant mollies are generally considered suitable.

Beak maintenance is essential: the fused teeth grow continuously and must be worn down through regular consumption of hard-shelled prey. Live ramshorn or bladder snails offered two to three times per week are the most practical solution. An overgrown beak will eventually prevent the fish from feeding and requires veterinary trimming under anaesthesia. Puffers produce considerable waste, so powerful filtration and frequent partial water changes (25–30% weekly) are important for maintaining water quality. This species should not be confused with the Green Spotted Puffer (Dichotomyctere nigroviridis), which reaches 15 cm, tends to be considerably more aggressive, and requires full marine salinity as an adult.

The older scientific names Tetraodon biocellatus and Tetraodon ocellatus still appear in much of the aquarium trade literature, but the current accepted name is Dichotomyctere ocellatus.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria is extremely rare but has been documented. The male selects or prepares a flat surface — a broad plant leaf or smooth rock — where the female deposits eggs. He then guards and fans the clutch until hatching, typically within five to seven days. Fry are minute and require live microscopic foods such as infusoria and rotifers for early growth, making successful rearing highly challenging. No widely accepted captive-breeding protocol has emerged for hobbyists. Brackish conditions and a heavily planted, species-only setup are generally considered prerequisites for any breeding attempt.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Top 10 Pufferfish You Can Keep in Freshwater and Brackish Aquariums
primary.notes: Primary trade/hobbyist source. Confirmed brackish requirement (SG 1.005–1.008), adult size 5–8 cm, temperature 22–26°C (72–79°F), alkaline pH, feeding on pest snails and frozen foods, and compatibility notes (bumblebee gobies, mollies). Tank size guidance: 20-gallon long minimum.

additional.name: FishBase — Dichotomyctere ocellatus (Eyespot Pufferfish)
additional.notes: Confirmed current accepted name Dichotomyctere ocellatus (Steindachner, 1870); max size 8.0 cm TL; temperature 22–26°C; native distribution Asia (Indochina, Malaysia, Indonesia); taxonomy Tetraodontidae, Tetraodontiformes; diet: snails and benthic organisms. FishBase lists as freshwater demersal but notes commercial aquarium trade use. Trophic level 3.4.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — In Search of the Peaceful Puffer
additional.notes: Described as 6 cm SE Asian species; ideal SG 1.005; greenish-brown with yellow figure-8 markings; juveniles more peaceful than adults; adults territorial fin-nippers; bumblebee gobies noted as safer tankmates. Treated as dwarf cichlid equivalent in temperament. Confirmed as Tetraodon biocellatus (synonym).

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Figure of Eight Puffer
additional.notes: Provided pH 7.2–8.2, hardness 12–25 dH, temperature 22–26°C, adult size 8 cm; native range Cambodia, Indonesia, Malaysia; diet: frozen/live mysis shrimp, mosquito larvae, brine shrimp, krill, cockle and mussel; breeding noted as extremely rare, eggs scattered among plants with male guarding; species-only tank recommended.

additional.name: Guidarium — Figure 8 Puffer (Brackish) Care Profile
additional.notes: Confirmed SG 1.005–1.008, pH 7.5–8.5, hardness 10–25 dGH, temperature 24–28°C, minimum 60 L / 60 cm tank; aggression level high (8/10); fin-nipper, snail eater, shrimp eater, territorial; best kept singly or species-only; sand substrate for foraging behaviour recommended.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is extremely rare. The male guards the egg clutch on a flat surface and fans it until hatching. Fry require live microscopic foods such as rotifers and infusoria. No widely accepted protocol for reliably breeding this species in a home aquarium has been established.
